claims.normalized_claim: Theranos was a blood-testing startup founded in 2003 by Elizabeth Holmes, a Stanford dropout, which claimed its proprietary 'Edison' device could run many laboratory tests from a single finger-prick drop of blood.
allowed_wording: Theranos, founded in 2003 by Stanford dropout Elizabeth Holmes, claimed it could run many tests from one drop of blood.

claims.normalized_claim: At its peak around 2014, Theranos was valued at roughly $9 billion, briefly making Holmes a paper billionaire; it partnered with Walgreens to offer testing in some stores (a limited rollout, mostly in Arizona), and its board included prominent public figures.
allowed_wording: At its peak it was valued near $9 billion; it put testing in some Walgreens stores and had a prominent board.
prohibited_wording: Theranos tests were in Walgreens nationwide.
claims.notes: Walgreens rollout was limited (mostly Arizona) — do not imply nationwide.

claims.normalized_claim: In October 2015, a Wall Street Journal investigation by reporter John Carreyrou reported that Theranos's technology did not work as claimed and that many tests were actually run on modified third-party commercial machines, with accuracy problems.
allowed_wording: In 2015 a Wall Street Journal investigation reported the technology did not work as claimed and that tests were run on other companies' machines.

claims.normalized_claim: Theranos collapsed and dissolved in 2018; the SEC charged Holmes with fraud in March 2018, and she settled the civil charges without admitting or denying wrongdoing, agreeing to a penalty and a ban from leading a public company.
allowed_wording: The company collapsed in 2018; the SEC charged Holmes with fraud, and she settled the civil case without admitting wrongdoing.
prohibited_wording: The SEC settlement was an admission of guilt.
claims.notes: SEC civil charge + no-admission settlement; distinct from the later criminal case.

claims.normalized_claim: In the federal criminal case United States v. Holmes, on January 3, 2022 a jury convicted Elizabeth Holmes on four counts of fraud against investors — one count of conspiracy to commit wire fraud and three counts of wire fraud.
allowed_wording: In January 2022 a jury convicted Holmes on four counts of defrauding investors — one conspiracy and three wire-fraud counts.
prohibited_wording: She was convicted on all counts.
She was convicted of defrauding patients.
claims.notes: STATE AS FACT: four investor-fraud convictions (jury verdict; upheld on appeal).

claims.normalized_claim: The same jury acquitted Holmes on the four counts related to patients (found her not guilty on those), and could not reach a verdict on three additional investor counts, on which a mistrial was declared.
allowed_wording: The jury found her not guilty on the four patient counts and could not agree on three other investor counts.
prohibited_wording: She was found guilty of harming patients.
The acquittals proved the technology worked.
claims.notes: PRECISION: 'acquitted' on patient counts; 'no verdict/mistrial' on 3 — NEVER 'guilty'. Acquittal != exoneration of conduct.

claims.normalized_claim: In November 2022, Holmes was sentenced to 135 months — about eleven years and three months — in federal prison, and she reported to prison in 2023.
allowed_wording: She was sentenced to about eleven years and three months in prison.
claims.notes: 135 months imposed. (A later good-conduct reduction is optional context, not required.)

claims.normalized_claim: Ramesh 'Sunny' Balwani, Theranos's former president and chief operating officer and Holmes's former partner, was separately convicted in 2022 on all twelve counts — including the patient-related counts — and sentenced to about 155 months, nearly thirteen years.
allowed_wording: Theranos's former president, Sunny Balwani, was convicted on all twelve counts, including the patient counts, and sentenced to nearly thirteen years.
claims.notes: Balwani convicted on patient counts (unlike Holmes); ~155 months.

claims.normalized_claim: Criminal fraud requires proof of intent to deceive, not merely optimism, ambition, or business failure; the split verdict — convicting Holmes of defrauding investors but acquitting her on the patient counts — illustrates the line the law draws between bold salesmanship and a crime.
allowed_wording: Fraud requires intent to deceive, not just failure; the split verdict shows where that line fell here.
prohibited_wording: The acquittals mean she did nothing wrong.
claims.notes: Framing claim; attribute the legal standard; do not editorialize beyond the record.

claims.normalized_claim: An acquittal on the patient counts means the prosecution did not prove those specific charges against Holmes beyond a reasonable doubt; it is not a finding that the technology worked or that patients were unharmed — a point underscored by Balwani's conviction on the patient counts.
allowed_wording: Being found not guilty on those counts means they weren't proven beyond a reasonable doubt — not that the tech worked; Balwani was convicted on the patient counts.
claims.notes: CRITICAL nuance for fairness; prevents 'acquittal = exoneration' misread.

findings: Durable hashes 4/4. R3 — pre-publish rights/legal review required. Convictions=fact; patient=acquitted; 3=mistrial; acquittal!=exoneration; attribute intent to jury/court; no likeness.
